ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general beef skewer food product entry; no specific spicy/mala variant exists. closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers grilled beef meat; no skewer-specific or mala-spiced code exists. closest_parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code exists for spiced beef skewers as a prepared food product.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of blazing beef skewers consists of 5 skewers, each holding approximately 25–30 g of marinated grilled beef, totalling roughly 150 g.

nutrient_source.title: Beef, ground, 85% lean meat / 15% fat, patty, cooked, pan-broiled (USDA SR Legacy 21140) adapted for marinated grilled skewer preparation with spice marinade adjustment
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Chinese spicy beef skewers exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy grilled lean beef (approximately 80–85% lean) with minor upward adjustment to sodium and fat to account for soy sauce and sesame oil marinade. Energy, protein, and fat are anchored to USDA FDC item 174036 (beef, ground, 85% lean, cooked, pan-broiled: 215 kcal, 26.1 g protein, 11.8 g fat per 100 g). Carbohydrate adjusted upward slightly (~2 g per 100 g) for marinade sugars and cumin/chili coating. Sodium adjusted to ~520 mg per 100 g to reflect soy sauce marinade. These are best-estimate values; a laboratory analysis of the specific restaurant product would improve precision.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chinese spicy beef skewers. Beef and other lean grilled meats are predominantly protein and fat with negligible carbohydrate content, yielding a very low GI. GI is estimated at 10 (low) by analogy with other grilled lean meat preparations reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables, where high-protein, low-carbohydrate meat dishes consistently fall in the GI 0–15 range. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (10 × 2.5 × 1.5) / 100 = 0.375, rounded to 0.4.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from soy sauce marinade (~520 mg per 100 g; ~780 mg per 5-skewer serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 2–3 skewers and avoid additional dipping sauces, as the soy sauce marinade contributes approximately 780 mg sodium per standard 5-skewer serving, representing roughly one-third of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (~2.5 g per 100 g) and low glycemic index (~10)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Blazing beef skewers are a low-carbohydrate, high-protein food with a glycemic index of approximately 10, making them suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderate portions; however, the sodium content from the marinade warrants attention in those with concurrent hypertension or diabetic nephropathy.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~4.3 g per 100 g) and sodium (~520 mg per 100 g) from beef and soy sauce marinade
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While beef skewers provide beneficial protein and iron, the saturated fat content (~6.5 g per 150 g serving) and elevated sodium from the marinade are relevant for individuals with cardiovascular disease, who should limit intake to 2–3 skewers per occasion and pair with non-starchy vegetables to improve the overall dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Blazing beef skewers (5 pieces) is a Chinese-style grilled meat dish consisting of bite-sized pieces of marinated beef threaded onto bamboo or metal skewers and cooked over high heat. The marinade typically includes soy sauce, sesame oil, cumin, dried chili flakes, garlic, and scallion, producing a bold, smoky, spicy, and slightly savoury flavour profile with a tender yet slightly charred texture. This dish is inspired by Chinese street-food chuanr (串儿) culture, particularly associated with Xinjiang and northern Chinese culinary traditions, and is widely adapted across Chinese restaurants in Singapore. Nutritionally, each 5-skewer serving (approximately 150 g) provides roughly 327 kcal, 38 g of protein, 17 g of fat, and only 3.8 g of carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, low-carbohydrate option. The glycemic index is very low (estimated ~10), which is favourable for blood glucose management. However, the sodium content is elevated (~780 mg per serving) due to the soy sauce marinade, which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or diabetic nephropathy. The dish is dairy-free and contains soy and sesame allergens. It is not certified halal or kosher due to the use of beef and potential cross-contamination in restaurant settings. Clinically, this dish is suitable for high-protein dietary patterns and low-carbohydrate meal planning, but sodium monitoring is advised for at-risk populations.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Chinese spicy beef skewers exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or any primary regional food composition database. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy grilled lean beef with marinade adjustments. GI is estimated by analogy with grilled lean meat preparations from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the adaptation is methodologically sound and anchored to a high-quality primary source, but laboratory verification of the specific restaurant product would be required for high confidence.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this food category and is not expected to be significant in grilled beef. All other -1 fields (monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g) are in fact populated with estimated numeric values derived from the USDA SR Legacy adaptation and are not sentinel -1 values. Only vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 as a sentinel. Halal status is listed as false because the restaurant (Lan Ting Xu Lanzhou Beef Noodles) is not confirmed halal-certified on field survey data at time of processing. Serving size of 150 g is estimated based on 5 skewers at approximately 25–30 g of beef per skewer, consistent with typical Chinese restaurant skewer portions. Glycemic load per serving = (GI 10 × carbohydrate_g per serving 3.75) / 100 = 0.375, rounded to 0.4.
